title: Cutting foam to size for upholstery
subtitle: Mark, score, compress, and tear; or use an electric carving knife
excerpt: How to cut CMHR upholstery foam to size for a seat pad or cushion: marking the cut line, choosing between a serrated knife and an electric carving knife, cutting cleanly without compressing the foam, and gluing the foam to a board or frame.
sourceNotes: City & Guilds Upholstery Level 2 syllabus. Foam cutting technique from David James A Complete Course in Upholstery (2014). CMHR foam fire-safety requirement from the Furniture and Furnishings (Fire) (Safety) Regulations 1988 (as amended).

recipeTools.notes: For straight cuts in thin foam up to 25 mm.
For trimming foam edges and dacron wrap.
Wrapped around the foam before the cover for a rounded finished look.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combustion-modified high-resilience polyurethane foam. UK regulations require CMHR foam in any upholstered furniture sold or repaired since 1988. Density and firmness graded; medium firmness for seats, firmer for arms.
A polyester-fibre wadding wrapped around upholstery foam before the top cover. Softens the edge of the foam and gives the finished cushion a slightly upholstered roundness.

content.text: UK regulations require
CMHR foam

in any upholstered furniture that has been sold or repaired since 1988. Always specify CMHR when ordering; the foam should carry the blue and yellow kite label confirming it meets the regulations. Foam for seats is typically 50 to 100 mm thick at medium or firm density; thinner foam is for arm and back pads.
Measuring
Step 1, measure the seat frame.

Measure the seat area at its widest and deepest points including any taper. For a straight-sided seat: cut the foam to the exact seat dimensions. For a taper: cut the foam slightly oversized and trim the taper after the straight cuts.
Step 2, mark the foam.

Mark the cut lines on the top surface of the foam using a carpenter's pencil, a felt-tip marker, or a ballpoint pen. Use a long metal rule or straightedge for straight cuts; for curves, draw freehand or use a template.
Cutting methods
Electric carving knife.

The quickest method for straight cuts in thick foam. Run the knife along the line in a single pass without pushing down; let the serrated blades do the work. The electric knife cuts perpendicular to the surface cleanly.
Utility knife for thin foam.
For foam up to 25 mm thick, a sharp utility knife against a metal rule cuts cleanly in two or three passes. Do not compress the foam while cutting; this produces an angled cut.
Score and compress method for thick foam.

Score deeply along the cut line with the utility knife to at least half the foam depth. Compress the foam at the score line and pull the two sides apart; the foam tears cleanly along the scored line. This method produces a very slightly convex cut edge, which is useful for edge-of-seat profiles.
Fitting the foam to the seat
Step 3, glue the foam to the seat board.

Apply a thin, even coat of upholstery contact adhesive (Evostik Impact or equivalent) to the top of the seat board and the bottom face of the foam. Allow both surfaces to go tacky for 3 to 5 minutes. Press the foam onto the board and apply firm pressure for 30 seconds. The foam should not be able to slide once bonded.
Step 4, wrap in Dacron if required.
Wrap the foam in a layer of
Dacron
and staple the wrap to the underside of the board. Dacron softens the edge of the foam and prevents the cover fabric showing the sharp foam edge as a visible line.
Common mistakes
Foam cut with angled sides rather than square.
The foam was compressed while cutting. Support the full length of the foam flat on the cutting surface and cut without pressing down.
Foam sliding on the board after gluing.
The adhesive was not allowed to go tacky before pressing. Separate the surfaces, apply fresh adhesive, wait the full 3 to 5 minutes, and re-press.
